voice.style: Warm, conversational, technically sharp, lightly funny, and paced for a 20-minute developer workshop.

slides.title: Agent Harnesses
slides.captionText: Agent harnesses are the repo and team system around Copilot: rules, moves, tools, and checks that make AI work bounded and reviewable.
slides.ttsText: Welcome to one oh two: Agent Harnesses. [pause] In the first session, we talked about the basics: context, tools, prompting, task selection, and how to steer a Copilot session. And if you did the loop workshop, you have already practiced the live rhythm: frame the task, watch the work, interrupt drift, and verify the result. [pause] This talk moves one level out. The question is not only, how do I personally use Copilot better? The question is, how do we make the repo and the team environment better, so every Copilot session starts with clearer boundaries and better checks? [long-pause] That surrounding system is what I mean by an agent harness. It is not one magic prompt. It is not one giant policy document. It is the practical stuff around the agent: instructions, skills, scripts, permissions, review habits, and verification commands. [pause] One way to think about it is this: Copilot is working inside your engineering system. If the system is vague, Copilot has more room to wander. If the system is explicit, Copilot has better rails. That does not make the agent perfect, but it makes mistakes smaller and easier to catch. [pause] The promise is simple. A good harness keeps Copilot useful, bounded, and reviewable. Useful, because it gives Copilot the repo knowledge it needs. Bounded, because it says what it should not touch. Reviewable, because it gives both the agent and the human a way to prove what happened. [pause] The goal is not to trust Copilot more blindly. The goal is to make the work easier to inspect.

slides.title: Prompting Is Not Enough
slides.captionText: Prompts shape one session. Harnesses preserve the durable repo knowledge, boundaries, and checks that every session should use.
slides.ttsText: Let us start with the trap. When Copilot makes a mess, the first reaction is usually: we need a better prompt. [pause] Sometimes that is true. A clearer task helps a lot. But better prompting only solves the current conversation. If every developer has to restate the package manager, test command, generated files, risky folders, and review expectations every time, then the repo is making people carry too much in their heads. [long-pause] A prompt is per-session context. A harness is durable context. [pause] The prompt says, here is the task right now. The harness says, here is how this repo works, here are the boundaries, here are the procedures, and here is how we check finished work. [pause] This matters because the model is always trying to complete the task from the context it has. If the durable context is missing, it fills the gap with patterns from the world. Sometimes those patterns are right. Sometimes they are the wrong framework, the wrong helper, the wrong command, or the wrong level of autonomy for your repo. [pause] That means the repo starts doing some of the teaching. Copilot does not have to guess whether generated files should be edited directly. It does not have to invent the test command. It does not have to assume migrations are safe to run. The harness gives it the boring, important facts that should be true every time. [pause] And boring is good here. Boring means fewer surprises in the diff. Boring means the human reviewer starts from evidence, not from a confident summary. So yes, write better prompts. But do not stop there. Put the repeatable knowledge somewhere the agent can actually see it.

slides.title: The Harness Model
slides.captionText: The simple model is Rules, Moves, Tools, Checks: what Copilot should follow, how work should proceed, what it can use, and how it proves results.
slides.ttsText: Here is the simple model for the rest of the talk: rules, moves, tools, checks. [pause] Rules are the standing instructions and boundaries. They tell Copilot how the repo works, what conventions matter, which areas are risky, and what it should ask before doing. [pause] Moves are the repeatable procedures. This is where skills and playbooks fit. How do we triage a bug? How do we review an auth change? How do we diagnose something after code generation? [pause] Tools are the capabilities the agent can use. In many repos, the most important tools are not fancy. They are scripts: run changed tests, collect a lint report, smoke test a local page, prepare review evidence. MCP can fit here too, but for this talk the practical center is whatever controlled capability helps the agent touch reality. [pause] Checks are the proof layer. Tests, typecheck, lint, soft warnings, diagnostic skills, manual QA, and the human review checklist. [long-pause] Notice that these four pieces answer different questions. Rules answer: what should be true? Moves answer: how do we usually do this kind of work? Tools answer: what can the agent safely run or inspect? Checks answer: how do we know it worked? When those questions are separated, the system is easier to improve. If Copilot fails, you can ask which layer was missing instead of treating the whole thing as one vague AI problem. [pause] If you remember nothing else, remember this distinction: instructions define the rules. Skills define the moves. Tools define the capabilities. Checks define the finish line. A good harness has all four, even if it starts small.

slides.title: Rules: Copilot Instructions
slides.captionText: Copilot instructions are standing orders for the repo: commands, conventions, boundaries, and what done means.
slides.ttsText: The most obvious place to start is Copilot instructions. Think of them as standing orders for the repo. [pause] In VS Code and GitHub Copilot, a repository can include files like dot github slash copilot instructions dot md. VS Code also supports agent-style instruction files like A G E N T S dot md and more specific instruction files. The names matter less than the job: give Copilot durable guidance it should not have to guess every session. [long-pause] Good instructions answer practical questions. What package manager do we use? What command runs tests? Are generated files edited directly, or regenerated? What helper do we use for API calls? Are there conventions for errors, logging, dates, environment variables, or accessibility? [pause] Then the important part: what are the boundaries? Maybe auth changes require regression tests. Maybe migrations need approval. Maybe public APIs should not move casually. Maybe production config and secrets are never touched. [pause] And finally, what does done mean? Not in a motivational poster way. In a useful way. Done means the focused tests passed. Done means the UI was checked in a browser. Done means the agent reports what it changed, what it verified, and what risk remains. [pause] The best instructions are short, concrete, and a little opinionated. They do not say, write clean code. They say, use this test helper. They do not say, be careful with migrations. They say, do not create or run migrations without approval. They do not say, follow our style. They name the local pattern the agent should copy. [pause] Keep these instructions short and specific. If a rule changes a decision, keep it. If it is just vibes, cut it.

slides.title: Rules: Boundaries and Blast Radius
slides.captionText: Boundaries are not only safety rules. They make agent work easier by narrowing what Copilot can inspect, change, run, and ship.
slides.ttsText: Boundaries can sound like a safety lecture, but they are also a productivity tool. [pause] When you tell Copilot what is allowed, what needs approval, and what should never happen alone, you are not just preventing disaster. You are narrowing the search space. [pause] I like to think in blast-radius levels. Reading files, docs, logs, and issues is usually low risk. Editing local code, tests, and docs is more serious, but still reviewable. Running commands depends on the command. A focused test is very different from a migration. And shipping actions, like deploys, releases, merges, production data changes, or secrets, need human gates. [long-pause] A repo harness can make this concrete with three buckets. Allowed: read the relevant files, add focused tests, update local code inside the task boundary. Ask first: install packages, delete files, change public APIs, run migrations, or touch shared config. Never alone: secrets, production data, releases, protected branches, and anything where rollback is not cheap. [pause] This also helps with review. If the task says edit one component and one test, and Copilot touches routing, auth, generated files, and CI config, the boundary violation is obvious. You can stop early. You do not need a philosophical debate about whether the model is smart. The harness already said the work left the box. [pause] The point is not to make Copilot timid. The point is to give it a safe operating area. Strong boundaries make the agent faster because it stops exploring places it should not go, and they make review calmer because the diff stays inside a box.

slides.title: Moves: Skills and Playbooks
slides.captionText: Skills and playbooks turn team procedures into reusable moves, such as plan review before coding or diagnostic review after generation.
slides.ttsText: Now the second part of the model: moves. [pause] Instructions say what should be true. Skills and playbooks say how to do repeatable work. [pause] This is where I think teams can get a lot of leverage. If you have a workflow you repeat, do not make every developer reinvent it in chat. Turn it into a reusable procedure. [long-pause] For example, before coding, you might use a grilling skill to stress-test the plan. What is the scope? What evidence is missing? What files are likely involved? What would make this risky? That is not code generation. That is decision hygiene before the agent starts changing files. [pause] After coding, you might use a diagnostic skill, like a feature diagnosis or bug-hunting procedure, to inspect what got generated. It can ask: did the change actually cover the behavior? Are there edge cases? Did tests check the right thing? Did the diff wander? [pause] This is different from a long prompt pasted from someone's notes app. A skill can be versioned, shared, improved, and reused. It can include a short procedure, references, and sometimes helper scripts. That means the team's way of doing the work becomes part of the environment instead of a private memory trick. [pause] The names can be simple: grill me, diagnose, review auth, UI smoke, migration safety, release notes. The exact skill system may vary by tool, but the idea is portable. A skill is a reusable move with instructions, maybe scripts, maybe references, that helps the agent follow the team's way of working. [pause] Instructions define the rules. Skills define the moves.

slides.title: Moves: Team Habits
slides.captionText: A harness should capture the team habits that make agent work reviewable: triage, migration safety, UI checks, and PR summaries.
slides.ttsText: This is where the harness becomes a team practice, not just a tooling trick. [pause] Every team already has habits. Some are written down. Some live in people's heads. Some only appear in pull request comments after something went sideways. [pause] An agent harness is a way to move the useful parts out of memory and into the repo. [long-pause] Bug triage is a good example. A team might expect evidence first: a repro, an error log, an owner, a likely surface, and one focused verification path. If that is the habit, write it down as a playbook. [pause] Migration safety is another. Dry-run first. Explain the data change. Get human approval before a destructive command. That should not depend on whether the person using Copilot remembers to say it. [pause] UI work has habits too. Run the app. Inspect the actual screen. Capture what changed. Do not claim accessibility is fixed without checking keyboard flow or labels. [pause] PR review has habits: summarize evidence, changed behavior, checks run, checks not run, and remaining risk. [pause] The hidden benefit is consistency. When the harness carries the habit, the junior engineer, the senior engineer, and the agent all start from the same basic operating model. People can still use judgment, but they are not starting from a blank chat box every time. [pause] If the team repeats a move, the harness should teach the agent that move.

slides.title: Tools: Scripts Agents Can Run
slides.captionText: Named scripts give Copilot safe, repeatable commands for changed tests, verification bundles, local smoke checks, and PR evidence.
slides.ttsText: Now tools. When people hear agent tools, they sometimes jump straight to MCP servers, databases, tickets, deployments, and complex integrations. Those can be useful. But for many teams, the highest-value tool is much simpler: a good script. [pause] A named script gives Copilot a safe path through verification. Instead of asking it to figure out your test strategy from scratch, you give it something like npm run test changed. Or npm run agent verify. Or scripts slash check pr dot sh. [long-pause] Changed-test runners are especially useful. If Copilot edits three files, the repo can help find the closest tests instead of running the entire suite or guessing. [pause] Verification bundles are useful too. An agent verify command can run typecheck, a lint report, focused tests, maybe a smoke check, and then return output the agent can summarize. [pause] The trick is to design these scripts for agent use. Clear names. Predictable output. Reasonable runtime. No destructive side effects. Good scripts should also fail or warn in ways an agent can understand. If the output is just a thousand lines of noise, it is technically information, but not very useful context. If the script groups failures and says what to inspect next, it becomes part of the harness. [pause] If a command is risky, make the script refuse to run without explicit human input. [pause] A script is not glamorous, but it is a very strong harness piece. It gives the agent a way to touch reality without wandering.

slides.title: Soft Checks: Warnings Before Hard Gates
slides.captionText: Soft checks are useful for repos without hard lint gates: return warnings so Copilot can clean up issues before human review.
slides.ttsText: Let us talk about soft checks, because this is a very practical middle ground. [pause] Some repos have strict linting, strict type rules, complete test gates, and everything blocks the merge. Great. If you have that, use it. [pause] But lots of real repos are messier. Maybe linting exists but is too noisy to block. Maybe TypeScript has old debt. Maybe accessibility checks are useful but not mandatory. Maybe a full suite takes too long for every agent session. [long-pause] That does not mean Copilot should get no feedback. It means the harness can return warnings instead of hard blocks. [pause] A heavy lint report can say: here are unused exports, suspicious any types, accessibility hints, risky patterns, slow tests, or a surprisingly wide diff. It can exit successfully so the session keeps moving, but the output gives Copilot something to fix or at least report. [pause] This is especially useful after generated code. The agent can run the soft report, clean up obvious issues, and then tell the human what warnings remain. [pause] Think of it as a pre-review radar. It may not decide mergeability, but it points attention. In a repo that does not have hard linting yet, this can be the bridge from no signal to useful signal. You get quality feedback without pretending the whole legacy codebase is suddenly clean. [pause] A soft check is not permission to ignore quality. It is a way to surface quality earlier, before the reviewer has to discover every issue by hand.

slides.title: Checks: After Generation
slides.captionText: After Copilot writes code, the harness should push it through changed tests, soft lint, diagnostic skills, and an evidence summary.
slides.ttsText: Here is the post-generation version of the harness. Copilot writes code inside the task boundary. Then it should not immediately declare victory. [pause] First, it runs the checks the repo gives it: changed tests, typecheck, soft lint report, maybe a local smoke command. The point is to get real output into the conversation. [pause] Second, it diagnoses the result. This is where a skill can be useful. Ask it to look for bugs, missing edge cases, weak tests, public API drift, or changes that do not match the original goal. This is not a replacement for human review. It is a pre-review pass that catches cheap mistakes before you spend attention on them. [long-pause] Third, it reports like an engineer. What evidence did it use? What changed? What checks passed? What warnings remain? What was not verified? What risk is left? [pause] This is also where the agent can be surprisingly useful. It can compare the original task to the diff. It can read the tests it added and ask whether they prove the right behavior. It can notice that a cleanup touched unrelated files. It can tell you the smoke check was not run because the app did not start. That is not perfect judgment, but it is much better than a final answer with no receipts. [pause] That summary is much more useful than: fixed it. [pause] Human review should start from evidence. If the agent cannot tell you what it inspected, what it changed, and what it verified, then the harness did not finish its job. Confidence is not the finish line. Checks are the finish line.

slides.title: The Harness Learning Loop
slides.captionText: When Copilot fails, improve the harness: add an instruction, script, test, boundary, skill, or review question.
slides.ttsText: The best part of a harness is that it can learn. Not magically. Practically. [pause] When Copilot fails, do not only ask, what should I have prompted differently? Ask, what did the repo fail to provide? [long-pause] If Copilot missed context, maybe the fix is a repo instruction or path-specific rule. If it ran the wrong command, maybe the fix is a named script. If it changed too much, maybe the fix is a stricter boundary or task template. If it claimed success with weak evidence, maybe the fix is a better test, smoke check, or review checklist. If it made the same kind of mistake twice, maybe the fix is a skill that catches that failure mode after generation. [pause] This is how teams get better without asking every developer to become a prompt wizard. The learning moves from private memory into shared repo structure. [pause] The important thing is to capture small lessons quickly. Do not wait for a huge process rollout. If a command is always the right command, document it. If a folder is always dangerous, add the boundary. If reviewers always ask the same question, put it in the checklist. Small harness improvements compound because they are reused every session. [pause] That is the loop: failure, cause, harness fix, next session. The next session starts smarter because the repo now carries a little more of the team's judgment. [pause] Do not only fix the prompt. Fix the system that shaped the prompt.

slides.title: Conclusion: Make Work Inspectable
slides.captionText: The goal is bounded, visible, checkable AI work: instructions and permissions bound it, skills and scripts reveal it, checks prove it.
slides.ttsText: So the wrap-up is simple. Agent harnesses are how teams make AI work inspectable. [pause] Bounded: instructions, path rules, permissions, and blast-radius decisions define where Copilot should operate and where it should stop. [pause] Visible: skills, playbooks, scripts, and diagnostic passes make the agent's path easier to understand. We can see what it inspected, what procedure it followed, and what command output it used. [pause] Checkable: tests, soft reports, smoke checks, review questions, and CI gates prove the work better than a confident summary ever will. [long-pause] This is the practical team move after learning the agent loop. The individual developer still matters. You still steer. You still review. You still use judgment. But the repo should help. [pause] And this can start small. One instruction file. One path-specific boundary. One changed-test script. One soft lint report. One diagnostic skill after code generation. That is already a harness. You do not need the perfect platform before you make Copilot safer and easier to review. [pause] If I were turning this into a first team action, I would not start with the fanciest integration. I would pick one painful repo area and ask: what does Copilot keep guessing here? Then I would add one rule, one repeatable move, one safe command, and one check. That is enough to change behavior. And after the next agent mistake, I would add one more small improvement. [pause] Over time, that becomes the difference between random AI sessions and a real engineering practice. The harness is how the team keeps its judgment close to the work. [pause] That is the part I want to leave you with: the agent is not separate from engineering discipline. It runs through whatever discipline the repo makes available. [pause] The goal is not to trust Copilot more. The goal is to make the work easier to inspect. If the work is bounded, visible, and checkable, the team can use agents with more speed and less chaos. That is the harness.
